title: The Complete Suit Tailoring Process — From Measurements to Pickup, Explained Step by Step
excerpt: First time getting a custom suit? No need to worry. We break down the entire process: measuring, fabric selection, fittings, and more.
content:
## Why More People Are Choosing Custom Suits

No matter how well an off-the-rack suit fits, it can never perfectly match every curve of your body. Everyone's shoulders slope differently, arm lengths vary slightly, and waistlines differ — these subtle variations are impossible for mass-produced garments to accommodate. The core value of custom tailoring is turning a standardized product into a piece made just for you. In recent years, as men's fashion awareness has grown, more and more guys are trying custom suits — from fresh graduates preparing for their first interview to business professionals seeking refined style. Tailoring is no longer just for the wealthy.

But many people find the process confusing: How many visits do I need to make? What measurements are taken? Which fabric should I choose? When do fittings happen? And why do prices range from a few hundred to tens of thousands? If you walk into a tailor's shop without understanding these things, you could easily be steered wrong — paying custom prices for what amounts to a rough copy of your measurements. Today I'll break down the entire custom suit process from start to finish, so whether you're visiting a tailor for the first time or looking to upgrade your experience, you'll know exactly what to expect. In the world of men's fashion, custom tailoring is the ultimate experience.

The starting point of custom tailoring isn't fabric selection — it's clarifying your needs. At the same price point, different uses demand completely different cuts and fabrics. If you're making your first suit for interviews and daily business, go with classic midnight blue worsted wool, single-breasted two-button with a notch lapel — the safest, most foolproof choice. If it's for a wedding, you can open up — double-breasted, peak lapels, even velvet are worth considering. For casual everyday wear, a cotton-linen blend with a more relaxed cut works better. Clarify the purpose first, then start the process — that's how you make sure every dollar is well spent.

## Step 1: Taking Measurements — The Foundation of Custom Tailoring

Measurements are the most critical step in custom tailoring, bar none. About 70% of a suit's quality depends on the accuracy of your measurements. A professional measurement session covers over twenty data points, including but not limited to: shoulder width, chest, waist, hips, jacket length, sleeve length, back width, arm circumference, neck, collar, pants length, waistband, thigh, calf, and hem circumference. A deviation of even one centimeter in any measurement can affect the final fit.

Several key measurements require special attention. Shoulder width is the easiest to get wrong — the correct method is to measure the straight-line distance from the outer edge of the left shoulder bone to the outer edge of the right shoulder bone, not along the curve of the shoulder. Many people miss the bone landmarks, resulting in measurements that are too large or too small. For chest measurement, wrap the tape around the fullest part of the chest, keep it level across the back, and allow about one finger's width of ease. Waist should be measured at the natural waistline — usually one to two finger-widths above the navel — not where you wear your belt, because trousers sit at the natural waist.

Measurements also address a problem that off-the-rack suits can't solve: asymmetrical body shapes. Most people have uneven shoulder heights, slight differences in arm length, or even uneven hips. A professional tailor records these asymmetries during measuring and compensates for them in the pattern. For example, if your left shoulder sits slightly higher than your right, the left shoulder pad will be made thinner so the finished garment looks symmetrical. This "corrective cutting" is the core value of custom tailoring — it's not just about taking measurements; it's about reshaping your silhouette with fabric.

One more important thing: wear the shirt and shoes you plan to wear with the suit when you go for measurements. Shirt thickness directly affects the ease allowance for chest and collar, while heel height determines pant length precision. Many first-timers show up in a t-shirt and sneakers, only to find the finished suit too tight or too long when worn with a dress shirt and leather shoes — all because the reference points were wrong during measuring. Remember this detail, and it'll save you an extra fitting trip.

## Step 2: Choosing Fabric — The Key to the Final Look

Fabric choice determines half of a suit's character. Fabric origins generally fall into four tiers: the top tier is British fabrics — Scabal, Holland & Sherry, Dormeuil — with rich heritage, top-tier texture and durability. Upper-mid tier is Italian fabrics — Loro Piana, Zegna, Vitale Barberis Canonico (VBC) — known for elegant softness. The best value is high-quality domestic fabrics — products from Nanshan Group or Sunshine Group are more than adequate for custom suits in the ¥2,000–¥4,000 range.

Fabric weight (GSM — grams per square meter) determines the suit's ideal season. For summer (above 25°C), choose 220–260 GSM — lightweight and breathable. For spring/fall (15–25°C), 260–300 GSM is the most versatile and commonly used range. For winter (below 15°C), go with 300–360 GSM — heavier and more structured, but don't go so heavy that movement becomes difficult. If you only want one suit for year-round use, 260–280 GSM worsted wool is sufficient.

Color and pattern selection also require some know-how. For a first custom suit, start with a solid color: midnight blue is the safest, grey second, black third. Solids are recommended because without any pattern interference, the tailoring speaks for itself. For your second or third suit, you can explore textured fabrics like fine stripes, checks, or herringbone. A useful tip: stand about 1.5 meters from a mirror to evaluate the fabric — that's the distance others see you from. A fabric that looks solid from afar but reveals texture up close will appear more dimensional than a plain solid.

There's also an unspoken rule about patterns: the higher your position, the more visible the stripe can be. Entry-level professionals shouldn't wear overly bold striped suits, while senior executives often sport visible stripes or checks — it's become a subtle code for status and experience. Of course, this is a traditional guideline. Men's fashion today is far more diverse, and younger guys wearing checked suits is perfectly common. The key is wearing it with confidence.

## Step 3: Confirming Details and Fittings

Once the fabric is chosen, your tailor will go over the details of the suit with you. These include lapel style, lapel width, pocket style, number and material of buttons, vent style, lining color, and shoulder design. The lapel is the most visible style element: notch lapels are the most classic and versatile, ideal for first-time tailoring; peak lapels are more formal and stately, suited for more advanced wearers; shawl lapels are typically used for tuxedos and casual styles. For a first custom suit, stick with notch lapels — they never go wrong.

Lapel width is a subtle but important detail. Narrow lapels (7–8 cm) suit slim, younger men with a more modern vibe. Standard lapels (9–10 cm) suit most Asian men and are the safest, most conservative choice. Wide lapels (10–11 cm) suit taller, larger men or those going for a retro look. One easily overlooked point: lapel width should harmonize with your face and body shape — a guy with a broad face and wide shoulders wearing narrow lapels will look unbalanced, and vice versa.

Custom suits typically involve two fittings. The first is the "canvas fitting" — the suit's structure is established but lining and details haven't been added yet. The tailor uses this fitting to adjust jacket length, sleeve length, shoulder width, and waistline. The second is the "finished fitting" — the suit is largely complete, and you check whether the collar lies flat, sleeves hang naturally, and there's no excess wrinkling when buttoned. A good tailor will have you perform natural movements during the fitting — raising your arms, sitting down, turning around. The real issues are the ones that emerge during everyday actions.

## Step 4: Pickup and Aftercare

When you receive your finished suit, don't rush out. Spend time in the fitting room checking each part against these criteria: when standing naturally, the shoulders should be smooth; when buttoned, no X-shaped pull lines across the chest; no horizontal or vertical wrinkles on the back; sleeves should hang so the shirt cuff shows about 1–1.5 cm; pants should be the right length — not bunching up and not showing your ankles. If anything is off, speak up on the spot. A good tailor will leave enough margin for minor adjustments.

The first time you wear a custom suit, the feeling is completely different from off-the-rack — it conforms to every contour of your body, with no extra space and no tight spots. When you turn, raise your arms, or sit down, the suit moves with you instead of pulling or restricting. That "second skin" experience is something you'll never get from a ready-made suit. It's also why most people who try real custom tailoring never go back to off-the-rack.

Finally, a word on pricing expectations. In China, entry-level custom suits (domestic fabric + craftsmanship shop) run roughly ¥1,500–¥3,000. Mid-range (imported fabric + independent tailor) is ¥3,000–¥8,000. High-end (top-tier fabric + master tailor) starts at ¥8,000–¥20,000 and up. For first-timers, my recommendation is a budget of ¥3,000–¥5,000. At this price point, you can get good imported fabric and a tailor with solid skill and experience. Expensive doesn't always mean right for you, but something too cheap rarely delivers the cut and quality that custom tailoring is known for. After all, the quality of men's fashion often comes down to how the fabric feels and how well the cut fits.
